routesCount.description:
The AlternativeRoutesCount parameter allows the user to request additional routes that are nearly as optimal as the best route.
For example, if AlternativeRoutesCount is set to 2, the router will return the optimal route and up to two alternative routes.
These alternative routes are the next best routes according to the routing algorithm’s criteria.
Please note that the actual number of alternative routes returned may be less than the AlternativeRoutesCount if there are not enough viable alternative routes.
Impact on Performance: Increasing the AlternativeRoutesCount can increase the computation time for the routing request, as the service needs to calculate more routes.
Default Value: If AlternativeRoutesCount is not specified, ORS will return only the optimal route.

weightFactor.description:
Maximum factor by which route weight may diverge from the optimal route. 
The default value of 1.4 means alternatives can be up to 1.4 times longer (costly) than the optimal route.

shareFactor.description:
Maximum fraction of the route that alternatives may share with the optimal route. 
The default value of 0.6 means alternatives can share up to 60% of path segments with the optimal route.

AvoidBorders.description:
Setting AvoidBorders to "all", router will calculate a route that tries to avoid crossing all borders.
Setting AvoidBorders to "controlled", router will calculate a route that tries to avoid crossing controlled borders.
Not specify this option, router will use the normal routing algorithm which may include crossing borders.
It’s important to note that avoiding borders might result in a longer route, both in terms of distance and time.
Also, the AvoidBorders option is not a strict constraint. That means router might still cross borders if there’s no reasonable alternative route.

returnInstructions.description:
If set to true, the response will include detailed instructions for each step of the route,
such as “Turn left on Main St.” or “Continue straight for 2 km.”
These instructions can be useful for navigation purposes,
providing the user with clear directions on how to follow the calculated route.

coordinates.description: The waypoints to use for the route as an array of longitude/latitude pairs in WGS 84 (EPSG:4326)

id.description: Arbitrary identification string of the request reflected in the meta information.

elevation.description:
Includes elevation data in the routing response.
This means that the service will consider the elevation changes along the route,
providing more accurate information about the terrain and elevation profile of the route.

geometrySimplify.description:
When set to true, the service reduces the number of points in the route’s geometry, which can help to make the route data more manageable and less complex.
This simplification process is particularly useful when you need to display the route on a map or when you want to reduce the amount of data being transmitted.
It helps to maintain the overall shape and path of the route while removing unnecessary details.

geometry.description:
When set to true, it includes the route geometry in the response.
This means that the service will return the detailed path of the route as a series of coordinates.
If geometry is set to false, the route geometry will not be included in the response,
which can be useful if you only need other information about the route, such as distance or duration, without the detailed path.

maneuvers.description:
includes detailed maneuver instructions in the routing response.
These instructions provide step-by-step guidance for navigating the route, such as turning left, right, or continuing straight at specific points.
Including maneuvers can be particularly useful for applications that require detailed navigation instructions, such as turn-by-turn navigation systems.

suppressWarnings.description:
Suppresses warning messages in the routing response.
This can be useful if you want to avoid receiving warning messages about potential issues or limitations related to the route calculation.
For example, if there are certain restrictions or conditions that might affect the route,
setting SuppressWarnings to true will prevent these warnings from being included in the response,
allowing you to focus on the main routing information.

roundAboutExits.description:
includes detailed instructions for navigating roundabouts in the routing response.
This means that the service will provide specific exit numbers and directions for each roundabout encountered along the route.
For example, instead of just saying “Enter the roundabout,” the instructions might say “Take the third exit at the roundabout,”
which can be very helpful for drivers to navigate roundabouts accurately.

profile.description: The profile which should be used for the route calculation.

InstructionFormat.description: Defines the format of the instructions provided in the response.

Metrics.description:
Specifies a list of returned metrics.
distance - Returns distance matrix for specified points in defined units.
duration - Returns duration matrix for specified points in seconds.

ResponseFromat.description:
Json: This is the standard JavaScript Object Notation format. It’s a lightweight data-interchange format that is easy for humans to read
and write and easy for machines to parse and generate1.
GeoJson: This is a format for encoding a variety of geographic data structures. A GeoJSON object may represent a geometry,
a feature, or a collection of features. It’s based on the JSON format and includes information about the geographic features,
including points (like locations), lines (like streets), and polygons (like countries)12.
Gpx: This stands for GPS Exchange Format. It’s a type of data format that’s used to describe waypoints, tracks, and routes.
Its tags store location data (like latitude and longitude) and other information like elevation, time, and temperature.

averageSpeed.description:
refers to the average speed assumed for a particular type of road or transport mode when calculating routes.
This value is used in the routing algorithm to estimate the travel times between different points on the map.

detourFactor.description:
a measure of the difference from a straight line from the start to the end in terms of distance.
For example, if a route has double the distance compared to a straight line from point A to point B,
it would have a DetourFactor of 2. This means that the route is twice as long as the direct path from the start to the end point.
It’s a useful metric for understanding the efficiency of a route.

percentage.description:
A way of expressing the length of each segment in relation to the total length of the route.
Can be useful for understanding the composition of the route, such as which segments are the longest or shortest,
or how a particular segment compares to the overall route.

avoidHighways.description:
Setting AvoidHighways to true, router will calculate a route that tries to avoid highways as much as possible.
Setting AvoidHighways to false or don’t specify this option, the router will use highways where it makes sense based on other routing preferences.
It’s important to note that avoiding highways might result in a longer route, both in terms of distance and time.
Also, the avoid highways option is not a strict constraint. That means router might still use highways if there’s no reasonable alternative route.

avoidTollways.description:
Setting AvoidTollways to true, router will calculate a route that tries to avoid toll roads as much as possible.
Setting AvoidTollways to false or don’t specify this option, router will use toll roads where it makes sense to do so based on other routing preferences.
It’s important to note that avoiding toll roads might result in a longer route, both in terms of distance and time.
Also, the tollways option is not a strict constraint. That means router might still use toll roads if there’s no reasonable alternative route.

avoidFerries.description:
Setting AvoidFerries to true, router will calculate a route that tries to avoid ferry routes as much as possible.
Setting AvoidFerries to false or don’t specify this option, router will use ferry routes where it makes sense to do so based on other routing preferences.
It’s important to note that avoiding ferries might result in a longer route, both in terms of distance and time1.
Also, the avoid ferries option is not a strict constraint. That means router might still use ferry routes if there’s no reasonable alternative route.

avoidCountries.description:
List of countries to exclude from matrix with driving-* profiles.
Can be used together with 'avoid_borders': 'controlled'.
[ 11, 193 ] would exclude Austria and Switzerland.
List of countries and application examples can be found https://giscience.github.io/openrouteservice/technical-details/country-list.
Also, ISO standard country codes cna be used in place of the numerical ids, for example, DE or DEU for Germany.

avoidPolygons.description:
You can define the areas to avoid as polygons or multipolygons in GeoJSON format.
Router will then calculate a route that tries to avoid these areas as much as possible.
It’s important to note that the AvoidPolygons option is not a strict constraint. That means router might still pass through the polygons if there’s no reasonable alternative route.

restrictUTurns.description:
If RestrictUTurns is set to true, router will force the route to keep going straight at waypoints, restricting U-turns even if U-turns would be faster.
Not specifying this option or set RestrictUTurns to false, router will allow U-turns at waypoints if it makes the route faster.

radiuses.description:
The radius array is used to specify the maximum search distance for nearby road segments for each waypoint in your route.
Each value in the array corresponds to a waypoint in your route.
The value is the maximum distance (measured in metres) router will search from the waypoint to find a nearby road segment.
If the value is -1, router will use the maximum possible search radius.
If only a single value is provider, that value will be used for all waypoints.
in the example of [200,-1,30], router will search within 200 metres of the first waypoint,
use the maximum possible search radius for the second waypoint,
and search within 30 metres of the third waypoint to find nearby road segments.
This feature allows you to control the precision of the route calculation around each waypoint.
For example, if a waypoint is in a dense urban area with many roads,
you might want to use a smaller radius to ensure the route uses a specific road.
Conversely, in a rural area, you might use a larger radius or -1 to ensure the route calculation
doesn’t fail if the exact waypoint is not located on a road.

RoutePreference.description:
This property can be set to one of three values:
fastest: This will return the quickest route, usually the one with the shortest travel time.
shortest: This will return the shortest route in terms of distance, which may not necessarily be the fastest.
recommended: This will return the route that the router recommends based on a combination of factors such as road type,
traffic conditions, and other factors.

RouteUnit.description: Specifies the unit distance to use.

ApiKey.description: API Key needed to access the endpoints. API Key is passed in the request header.
ApiKey.name: X-API-KEY
